summary: Gruber responds to Joe Trotter's observation that classy blogs tend to run on Movable Type while WordPress blogs look generic. Gruber argues this conflates software with templates — what makes a site distinctive is original design, not the CMS behind it. He points to Zeldman and Matt Mullenweg as examples of WordPress sites with strong, unique identities. He then details his own design process for Daring Fireball, emphasizing that he started from a blank slate rather than default templates, carefully designing everything from layout to URL structure. The core argument is that default templates are fine for people who don't want to design, but terrible starting points for establishing a unique brand. True design means thinking about every detail, including things most people overlook like URL structure.
key_points: The perceived quality gap between Movable Type and WordPress sites is about original design versus default templates, not the underlying software
Gruber designed every aspect of Daring Fireball from scratch — visual design, markup, and URL structure — starting with blank files rather than default templates
URLs are a design element deserving careful thought: DF's /year/month/slug pattern was deliberately chosen for clarity, context, and minimal cruft
Starting from nothing forces you to think about everything, which is why sites built on default templates rarely achieve a distinctive identity
The CMS powering a site should be as invisible to readers as whether a magazine was laid out in QuarkXPress or InDesign
money_quotes: What makes Design Observer so good isn't that it feels Movable Type-y, but that it feels Design Observer-y.
It's just HTML and CSS; the software that generates it should be no more noticeable or relevant to you, the reader, than is whether a magazine was laid out using QuarkXPress or InDesign.
I did not design Daring Fireball by starting with one of Movable Type's default templates. I started the visual design with a blank sheet of paper, and then moved on to an empty Photoshop file.
Designing is thinking, and if you care about design, every detail — the layout, the colors, the markup, the URL structure — deserves to be thought about.
Default templates are terrific for people who can't or don't want to design their own — but they're terrible starting points for anyone attempting to establish their own unique brand.
If you start with nothing, you're forced to think about everything.
It took me six weeks to choose the exact shade of Daring Fireball's background color, #4a525a.
key_insight: A distinctive website comes from original design thinking applied to every detail — layout, markup, URLs — not from the choice of CMS, and starting from a blank slate forces the intentionality that default templates preclude.
